title: Voyager Stock Surges on NASA Moon Mission Contracts. 2 Others Fall.
content:
Tuesday, NASA announced “the selection of three companies to land four new missions on the Moon in late 2028 as part of the agency’s Moon Base Program.”  Astrobotic,  Firefly Aerospace  and  Intuitive Machines  won the business.  The trio will deliver science payloads to the lunar surface, part of NASA’s plan to build a permanent American presence on the Moon.

Continue Reading

title: SpaceX Got a Buy Call From a Tesla Bull. Why the Stock Plummeted.
content:
With shares of Elon Musk’s rocket and AI company volatile in post-IPO trading, investors will be paying close attention to what Wall Street has to say about valuation.  The latest rating is a bullish call, valuing SpaceX at a cool $2.5 trillion.  Tuesday evening, Wedbush analyst Dan Ives launched coverage of SpaceX with a Buy rating and a $190 price target.

Continue Reading

title: Why this analyst thinks it's too soon to buy SpaceX
content:
KeyBanc Capital Markets Senior Research Analyst, Aerospace & Defense, Michael Leshock, explains why he believes SpaceX (SPCX) is not a buy right now, adding that investors should wait to invest until some sizable milestones are met before diving into the stock.

Video Transcript

00:00 Josh

Well SpaceX is now one of the most important names for investors to watch, but Keybank's new call says dominance alone may not be enough to make the stock a buy. The firm initiating coverage on SpaceX with a sector weight rating. Joining me now is one of the analysts behind that call, Michael Leshock, Senior Research analyst of Aerospace and Defense at Keybank Capital Markets. Michael, it is good to see you.

00:32 Josh

So, you know, reading through your note, Michael, you sounded, you know, generally positive on SpaceX, but you do initiate at sector weight. So you're on a hold, you're on the sidelines. How come, Michael? Why why did you tell clients SpaceX is not a buy here?

00:54 Michael Leshock

Yeah, Josh, thanks for having me. So, from a valuation perspective, it's still there's still a lot of risks right now. So we're waiting to see some of recent milestones or or upcoming milestones rather for Starship to be addressed before we get more positive on the name.

01:21 Michael Leshock

Um, there are some lock-up periods in the stock, you know, systematic buying and selling, indexes, matching shares over the next year. Um, so there's there's a number of things that create this uncertainty and of course, the Elon premium as well. So, um, that creates a bit of arbitrariness in terms of the valuation of the stock and we prefer to to wait for some of these new starship milestones uh to be addressed before we get more positive.

01:43 Josh

Do you believe, Michael, the TAM, the uh the total addressable market that that SpaceX says it's attacking? They put that bogey at 28.5 trillion. I mean, you saw that, Michael. Do you believe that?

02:02 Michael Leshock

I think there's a lot of unknowns with the stock. I I I'm not saying it's it's out of the question over the long term, but if you look at that number, I mean that's 20% of global GDP. So, um it's a big number. I I think maybe someday they'll get there, but for the next couple of years, we don't see that as a realistic Ta uh that they'll be addressing. This is probably a decade plus down the road.

02:20 Josh

Do you think Michael, SpaceX ultimately kind of almost trades like Tesla in the sense that the narrative or the story almost becomes bigger than the fundamentals, bigger than traditional valuation metrics?

02:41 Michael Leshock

Absolutely. Um and that that goes right back to the Elon Premium. I mean, a lot of investors in both, you know, SpaceX and Tesla, uh believe in Elon and and that's what drives the premium valuation. and if you look at some of the performance milestones for for SpaceX, you know, it's there's there's some that are putting uh making a city on Mars with a million plus inhabitants.

03:07 Michael Leshock

So, uh that's something that's a little that's quite quite actually out of this world. Um and so this is what investors are betting on. You know, there's there's definitely a group of investors that are looking at these longer-term aspirations uh for really for all of humanity rather than, you know, being laser focused on a fundamental valuation construct. So that's the Elon premium and that's what's uh that's that's why we're on the sidelines as we wait for more of this price discovery really to be to be shaken out.

03:37 Josh

When you came out Michael and you told clients, all right, we're going to put the equivalent of a hold on this one. Did you have any pushback? I mean, did any clients say, okay, well, how could you, how could you be wrong, Michael? Like what what what could be some risks to the upside to think about?

03:52 Michael Leshock

I think from a valuation perspective, there wasn't a ton of pushback. Um I think they understand that, you know, from our perspective, we we like the story. We believe in in the company. They're clearly dominating the space industry and really even from a national security standpoint, I don't think the US Space administration would be where it is today without SpaceX. Um, and so they they have a great business.

04:22 Michael Leshock

They've proved that they hold the keys to space, which is uh our our key um watch point for SpaceX, you know, they prove that with Falcon 9, they have access to space. Now they need to prove it with starship. Um, so it comes back to the valuation and why we're sector weight.

04:39 Michael Leshock

Um there are a lot of key milestones and you look at building the largest rocket ever, which is starship, um that that has not been done before, you know, not even close. So, um, they're doing it, they're gradually working their way to ramping launch cadence, but until we get some reusability and much higher launch cadence, uh that's that's what we require to get more positive on the name.

05:07 Josh

Michael, let's switch and and talk about Rocket Lab as where as well because that's one where you sound more bullish. Why? What what makes you more confident in that name, Michael?

05:20 Michael Leshock

Yeah, so that's another company that holds the keys to space. Rocket Lab has launched over 90 times successfully and it's the much smaller rocket versus the Falcon 9 or a Starship. Um, but they have done it successfully many times and they're working on a larger rocket that would compete with Falcon 9 roughly in that medium lift launch uh bucket.

05:46 Michael Leshock

So, we really like the company, not just from a launch perspective, but also the space system, so satellite manufacturing and design, um, that segment is growing like a weed right now and then just this week, they bought Idium to bring themselves into the satellite constellation world and and ultimately, uh shift potentially to a services model over time. Um, and this is, this is not necessarily directly competing with SpaceX's starling.

06:17 Michael Leshock

But this is being uh high-speed, high reliability uh communications over time or across the globe. So it's it's very highly reliable spectrum that they got from this Idium deal. Um, and and we think it's great for Rocket Lab.

06:40 Josh

You also talk in the note um about Rocket Lab's vertical integration. why does that uh matter so much that that Rocket Lab is is building a lot of its tech in house?

06:54 Michael Leshock

Yeah, vertical integration is a key within the space industry and that's also what what's made SpaceX so successful. So, they're not reliant on, you know, a number of suppliers to uh deliver for ahead of a launch. They they are Rocket Lab particularly is about 90% vertically integrated on the launch side.

07:23 Michael Leshock

So if a customer comes to them and and wants to launch in, you know, three or six months from now, they can do it without relying on the rest of the supply chain. So, um these key events like a rocket launch are what drive the success in this industry and being being reliable is what the customer base is looking for across commercial space companies. Um if if you are too reliant and I think that's an issue.

07:51 Josh

You also talked about Michael, kind of a broader point, but it's an interesting one. You talk about how NASA you say is accelerating initiatives now at a pace not seen since the Apollo era. Um, you know, think of just explain that Michael, we sort of entering in your opinion, are you suggesting kind of a new golden age here of of space development?

08:16 Michael Leshock

Absolutely. When Jared Isaacman took over in late 25, ever since then, there have been, you know, executive orders, NASA press releases, awards being given, uh just yesterday, there were awards for Lunar Landers for Astrobotic, which is Voyager, recently bought them, uh Firefly,

08:35 Michael Leshock

uh as as well as intuitive machines. So, these are monthly announcements that NASA has said they're going to do and we expect more awards to come uh over the next coming months because they've talked about having uh 20 to 30, 20 plus lunar Landers between 2027 and 2029 during this first phase of building the moon base. So, this is coming, it's coming very fast.

09:02 Michael Leshock

And NASA has really taken the initiative here to quickly and efficiently build out the moon base with the help of commercial space players.

09:12 Josh

Michael, great to have you on the show today. Thanks for your time.

09:16 Michael Leshock

Thanks so much.

View Comments

title: Musk Meme Traders Rush Back Into Tesla When the Stock Hits $420
content:
(Bloomberg) -- Crossing $420 gets Tesla Inc. investors excited.

Most Read from Bloomberg

Meta Is Planning a Cloud Business to Sell AI Computing Power SpaceX IPO Left Mirae With No Shares on Misunderstanding Krafton Agrees to Pay 'Subnautica 2' Bonuses as Developer's CEO Resigns US Decides Against Renewing USMCA, Shifting to Rolling Talks Trump Reports at Least $1.4 Billion in 2025 Crypto Earnings

At least, that's what data from Vanda Research show: The sum of five-day retail net buying picks up on average from $87 million to $135 million whenever the price moves above the cannabis-linked level, Vanda global macro strategist Viraj Patel said.

The number 420 allegedly first came to be associated with cannabis in the 1970s and, according to one account, marked the point in the afternoon when a group of California teens convened to get high. It's since become a global signifier of smoking — and a favorite joke of Tesla CEO Elon Musk and the legion of investors who mobilize around him and share his adolescent bro brand of humor.

"I can't believe I'm actually writing this, but it really comes down to the same two magic numbers, $420 and $69, that get the meme crowd going," Roundhill Investments Chief Executive Officer Dave Mazza said. "Bitcoin's push to $69,000 became a target in itself. GameStop traders latched onto round numbers as milestones to screenshot."

On Tuesday, Tesla stock ended the session at $420.60. It had crossed that threshold, on the way up and down, several times over the past few years. But it was the first time its closing price has actually featured the number since going public 16 years ago.

X, the platform formerly known as Twitter — owned by Musk's other public company, SpaceX — was alight with jokes and memes. One user bemoaned that shares didn't close nine cents higher.

The chief strategist at Interactive Brokers, Steve Sosnick, said he's surprised it took until now for Tesla to see a $420 close. "It's been a Musk-related meme for years."

And though it would be counterproductive for long investors to will a stock to perpetually remain at a certain price, regardless of what that price is, that hasn't stopped $420 from recurring.

The latest push over it came along with a broader stock market rebound. By Wednesday afternoon, it had jumped to around $426 ahead of the carmaker's second-quarter delivery report, which is expected Thursday.

"The technicals back it up, all the major moving averages reclaimed with momentum turning higher, so the meme number has strength behind it," said Roundhill's Mazza.

Story Continues

Still, Tesla retail flows were actually lower on Wednesday, so the 420 buzz hasn't kicked in yet. But the five-day averages show investors snapped up more Tesla stock in nine out of 14 instances of the stock crossing above $420 since mid-2024, Vanda's Patel said.

In 2018, Musk triggered a frenzy when he tweeted "Am considering taking Tesla private at $420. Funding secured." He rounded the stock price from a valuation calculation to that number because he said his girlfriend would find it funny. Tesla stock shot up 11% following the comment, but when it became clear that there was no actual take-private effort in effect, the shares fell, resulting in a securities fraud suit from the SEC.

For better or for worse, $420 has been a longstanding part of the Tesla narrative, according to Karobaar Capital's Haris Khurshid, and the stock officially closing there for the first time is just another chapter in the story.

"Tesla has one of the most engaged retail investor bases in the market, so numbers like that tend to take on a life of their own," Khurshid said. "If enough people trade around a level it becomes real regardless of why it started."

--With assistance from Felice Maranz.

Most Read from Bloomberg Businessweek

Spain Built Too Much Solar. Investors Want Out 'Southern Squeeze' Grips US Cities Once Known for Affordability The Wall Street Women Who Traded Finance Careers for Influencer Success Vuori CEO's First Two Startups Failed. This One Became a $5.5 Billion Brand ByteDance Picks Brazil for Its Largest Data Center Outside China

title: Leveraged SpaceX ETFs Are Exploding in Popularity. That's Usually a Warning Sign.
content:
Space Exploration Technologies (NASDAQ: SPCX) had the largest, most anticipated IPO in history, turning Elon Musk into the world's first trillionaire. Many investors piled into SpaceX ahead of the IPO to get a piece of the action, which has also boosted demand for leveraged SpaceX ETFs.

When leveraged ETFs explode in popularity, it's time to take a step back and reassess the stock market. It's a red flag when a lot of money rushes into leveraged ETFs, which can have consequences for broader market participants.

Missed Nvidia in 2009? This Rare Signal Is Flashing Again. In 2009, a "Double Down" signal flashed for a little-known chipmaker called Nvidia. For the first time in years, that same "Total Conviction" signal is flashing for a company 1/100th the size of Nvidia. Continue »Image source: Getty Images.

Leveraged ETFs tap into greed and euphoria

Leveraged ETFs fulfill their objective nicely. They provide 2x or even 3x exposure to a stock. Some leveraged ETFs offer additional exposure to upside movements, while others give you more exposure to a short position.

You can't be a risk-averse investor if you buy leveraged ETFs, which is part of the problem. The people who buy leveraged ETFs are willing to take substantial risks that work well in a bull market but hurt tremendously in a bear market.

Also, remember the nature of leveraged ETFs. They are financial products that provide leverage, and someone comfortable with these funds may also trade options and borrow money on margin. Too much of that activity turns the stock market from a steady engine of growth into a house of cards due for a big dip, and margin accounts are heading in that direction.

Margin debt increased by 53.7% year over year to reach a record $1.42 trillion. It's the second consecutive monthly increase as investors shrug off the conflict in Iran, and an 8.5% sequential growth rate on top of that.

Broad margin unwinds can be deadly

Bulls make money, bears make money, and pigs get slaughtered. The saying captures what happens when margin starts to unwind during the correction: Accelerated losses always start with the slaughters.

Leveraged ETFs can be a gateway into margin accounts, options, and other high-risk assets. An investor who is deep into margin and leveraged ETFs will be forced to sell some of their shares at a permanent loss to fulfill margin call requirements. A broker can sell stocks without asking for your permission and at any time if you have a margin account.

A 5% dip is enough to force margin calls on the most euphoric investors, and their forced selling can trigger a deeper dip that forces them to sell even more shares. Their selling puts downward pressure on stocks and may force people with high margins but who are slightly more cautious to sell some of their positions.

Story Continues

This cycle will continue until investors who use leveraged financial products sell a significant amount of their holdings so they do not have to sell anything else. Some people get out of the margin entirely after seeing the losses they incurred in a short period of time. Then, the stock market has an easier path to recovery.

What should investors do in this situation?

Leveraged ETFs and margin don't go up forever. When they lose value, investors are forced to sell funds and assets that they planned to hold for a long time. Even if they hold their positions, high expense ratios and margin rates on leveraged ETFs eat away at the portfolio.

Investors who do not use these financial products will still be affected, as it will show up in volatility. Leveraged ETFs amplify price movements of underlying stocks and can introduce traders to risky products like margin and options that increase leverage. What would normally be a 1% price swing may turn into a 2% or 3% price swing, especially for growth stocks.

Even as financial markets become more complex and funds offer more nuanced offerings, the path to success remains the same. Focus on stocks with solid fundamentals that you would be comfortable holding for the next decade. A broad market correction does not change the fundamentals of your favorite stocks.

The extended market corrections leverage allows savvy investors to buy their favorite stocks at lower prices. SpaceX leveraged ETF demand shows we are in a euphoric, greed-driven cycle that will eventually correct, but that's no reason to sell everything and panic.

Should you buy stock in Space Exploration Technologies right now?

Before you buy stock in Space Exploration Technologies, consider this:

The Motley Fool Stock Advisor analyst team just identified what they believe are the 10 best stocks for investors to buy now… and Space Exploration Technologies wasn't one of them. The 10 stocks that made the cut could produce monster returns in the coming years.
